Q1: I tried to keep things organized, but honestly, my routine was pretty loose. I'd set aside about 12 hours a week for studying, usually spread across a few sessions. I prefer reading and writing things out, so I'd download materials and work through them at my own pace. The structure was there in theory, but some weeks I just couldn't bring myself to stick to it. My anxiety often made it hard to start, even when I had time blocked out.
Q2: My pattern varied quite a bit depending on how I was feeling and what deadlines were coming up. I'd try to be consistent, but with eight online courses to juggle, things got chaotic. Some weeks I'd study more intensely before assignments were due, then barely touch anything afterward. I'm 27, so I've got other responsibilities too, and that meant my schedule shifted around a lot.
Q3: Usually it was assignments that got me to log in. I'd check the LMS to see what was due or to submit work. Sometimes I'd log in just to see if there were any announcements or if my peers had posted something in the discussions. The peer support aspect actually helped motivate me occasionally, knowing others were going through the same stuff.
Q4: There were stretches where logging in became automatic, especially mid-semester when assignments were rolling in consistently. I'd check in every couple of days just to stay on top of things. But it never felt truly routine—more like a habit born out of necessity rather than genuine engagement. When my anxiety spiked, even that minimal routine would fall apart.
Q5: The reading materials and written resources were most useful to me since that's how I learn best. I also found the discussion forums helpful when peers shared their perspectives or explained concepts differently. The peer interactions, even just reading what others wrote, made me feel less alone in struggling with the material. Videos were okay, but I'd often just skim through them.
Q6: I tended to skip or delay watching the longer videos unless they were absolutely essential. My attention would drift, and I'd end up rewinding constantly, which felt like wasted time. Some of the optional readings I ignored too, especially when I was feeling overwhelmed. If something wasn't directly tied to an assignment, it often got pushed to the bottom of my list.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to an assignment or helped me understand something I was stuck on. Practical application mattered—if I could see how it would help my grade or my understanding, I'd engage with it. Peer discussions sometimes felt valuable too, especially when someone explained something in a way that clicked for me.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't always afford. With my anxiety, I had to prioritize what was absolutely necessary to pass. Required activities had a sense of urgency that optional ones lacked. Sometimes I'd feel guilty about skipping optional content, which just added to my stress, but I had to make choices about where to spend my limited energy.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded most of the readings and PDFs to study offline. I'd print things out sometimes and annotate them by hand, or type up notes in my own documents. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the LMS—at my desk with a cup of coffee, working through printed materials. That's where the real studying happened for me.
Q10: There were many times I'd spend hours reviewing downloaded notes or rereading printed materials without touching the LMS at all. I'd work through practice problems on paper or discuss concepts with a study partner in person. None of that showed up anywhere online. My exam prep especially happened offline—I'd consolidate everything into written summaries.
Q11: Yes, frequently. I'd download a PDF or reading once, maybe skim it online initially, then spend much more time with it offline. The LMS would just show one quick access, but I might have read that document three or four times over the following days. My actual engagement was way deeper than what the system could capture.
Q12: There were definitely low points, especially when my anxiety was high. I'd feel paralyzed, unable to even open the LMS because I was worried about what I'd find or how behind I might be. Feeling sad made it hard to care about coursework. During those times, even simple tasks felt monumental. The peer support helped pull me back sometimes, but not always.
Q13: Fatigue and anxiety were the biggest factors for me. When I was mentally exhausted, I'd log in but just stare at the screen without really processing anything. Competing responsibilities—work stuff, personal life—definitely ate into my study time. Some weeks I'd barely manage to submit assignments on time, let alone engage deeply with the material.
Q14: More often than I'd like to admit. I'd have the LMS open, maybe a video playing or a reading visible, but my mind would be somewhere else entirely. I'd be worrying about other things or just feeling too drained to focus. I'd technically be 'active' but getting nothing out of it. Those sessions felt hollow and frustrating.
Q15: I postponed things constantly, especially when my anxiety was bad. Starting felt like the hardest part—I'd know an assignment was important but convince myself I'd do it later. Fear of doing it wrong or not understanding made avoidance feel safer in the moment. Then the deadline would loom, and I'd rush through in a panic.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my primary motivator. Without them, I probably wouldn't have engaged much at all given my low motivation. I'd log in more frequently as due dates approached, cramming everything into the last few days. It wasn't healthy, but the pressure was the only thing that consistently got me moving.
Q17: Feedback had a mixed effect. Positive feedback gave me a small boost, but negative feedback or criticism would send my anxiety through the roof. Sometimes I'd avoid checking grades for days because I was scared of what I'd see. When feedback was constructive and specific, it did help me adjust my approach for the next assignment.
Q18: The course structure was fairly flexible, which was both good and bad for me. Good because I could work at my own pace, bad because without strict accountability, I'd drift. Having clear deadlines helped, but the open-ended nature of some modules made it easy to procrastinate. More structure might have helped me stay on track.
Q19: The peer interaction components actually made engagement easier—knowing others were there helped with the isolation. But the sheer volume of content across eight courses made it hard to keep up. Some modules were better organized than others; when things were clearly laid out, I engaged more. Confusing navigation or unclear expectations made me want to avoid the LMS entirely.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is anxious or uncertain about what to do. They might log in repeatedly, click around, watch videos, but feel too paralyzed to actually submit work. Or they could be perfectionists who keep revising but never feel ready to turn things in. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity.
Q21: That definitely reflects my experience to some extent. My exam score wasn't great, but I passed because my assignment work was solid. A lot of my preparation happened offline, so my LMS activity looked lower than my actual effort. Someone could be deeply engaged through external resources and still show minimal online presence.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you log in, what you click on, submission times. They're decent at tracking completion and basic activity. But they completely miss the quality of engagement, offline studying, emotional state, and whether someone actually understood the material. They can't capture anxiety, sadness, or the mental effort behind each login.
Q23: For me, being engaged means actually understanding and thinking about the material, not just going through the motions. It's when I'm curious, asking questions, making connections. It's also about feeling connected to the course community, even if that's just reading peers' posts. True engagement involves my mind being present, not just my browser.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much my anxiety affected every interaction with the course. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent studying printed materials or the emotional weight of each assignment. It couldn't show the peer support conversations that happened outside the platform, or how sad I felt some weeks. The human side of my experience is invisible to the data.
